پریکشا.e: Learned borrowing from Classical Sanskrit परीक्षा (parīkṣā).

پریم.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit प्रेमन् (preman) through Old Hindi प्रेम (prema).

senses.g: neighbour (living next-door or adjacent to land)

پڑوسی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀟𑀺𑀯𑁂𑀲𑀺𑀅 (paḍivesia), from Sanskrit प्रतिवेश्य (prativeśya). By surface analysis, پَڑوس (paṛos, “neighbourhood”) + ی (ī).

senses.g: to read out (aloud; such a notification, announcement etc.)
to recite (ie. a religious or sacred poem or verses of the Quran etc.)

پڑھنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀠𑀇 (paḍhaï), from Sanskrit पृथ् (pṛth).

senses.g: dejection, numbness, sadness, palled, withered, down

پس منظر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پس منظر. By surface analysis, پس + منظر.

ex.u: لڑکیوں کے لئے پستان کا ابهار، جوانی کے آغاز کا حصّہ ہے۔
ex.r: Laṛkiyõ ke liye pistān kā ubhār, javānī ke āġāz kā hissā hai.
ex.e: For girls, the emergence of breasts is part of the onset of adolescence.

پستان.e: PIE word *pstḗn Borrowed from Classical Persian پستان (pistān, “breast; udder”).

پستہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پسته (pista), from Middle Persian pstk' (pistag).

senses.g: having the color of shelled pistachios; pistachio green

پستہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پسته (pista), from Middle Persian pstk' (pistag).

senses.g: having the color of [shelled] pistachios; pistachio green

پسر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پِسَر (pisar), پُسَر (pusar). Cognate of Sanskrit पुत्र (putra). Doublet of پُوت (pūt) and پُتْر (putr).

پسلی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀁𑀲𑀼𑀮𑀺𑀆 (paṃsuliā), from Sanskrit पर्शु (parśu), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *párćuš, from Proto-Indo-European *pérḱus.

پسند.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَسَنْد (pasand). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi پسند (psnd /⁠pasand⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian پَسَنْد (pasand). First attested in c. 1649 as Middle Hindi پسند (psnd /⁠pasand⁠/).

senses.g: Pasni (a city in Gwadar district, Balochistan, Pakistan)
Passu (a village in Hunza district, Gilgit-Baltistan, Pakistan)

پسینہ.e: Inherited from Prakrit *𑀧𑀲𑀺𑀡𑁆𑀡 (*pasiṇṇa) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-), from Sanskrit प्रस्विन्न (prasvinna).

senses.g: Peshawar (a city, the provincial capital of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)

پشاور.e: Corrupted from Classical Persian پَرَشَاوَر (parašāvar), ultimately from Sanskrit पुरुषपुर (puruṣapura). Cognates Pashto پېښور (pex̌awar) and Punjabi پِشَور (piśaur).

پشتو.e: Borrowed from Pashto پښتو (paẍto), likely influenced by Persian پشتو (paštu).

senses.g: Pushkalavati (the ancient capital of Gandhara kingdom, near Charsadda in modern Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan)
Pishin (a city in Pishin district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

پکا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀓𑁆𑀓 (pakka), from Sanskrit पक्व (pakva). Cognate with Gujarati પાકું (pākũ), Nepali पक्का (pakkā), Punjabi ਪੱਕਾ (pakkā) / پَکّا (pakkā).

پکارنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸𑀭𑁂𑀤𑀺 (pukkāredi), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀧𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀸𑀭𑁆- (*pukkār-, “shout”), ultimately onomatopoeic.

پکڑنا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀧𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀟𑁆- (*pakkaḍ-). Cognate to Gujarati પકડવું (pakaḍvũ), Marathi पकडणे (pakaḍṇe). Further etymology is uncertain, but it may derive from Sanskrit प्रकृष्टयति (prakṛṣṭayati, “to catch”) or प्रगृह्णाति (pragṛhṇāti, “to hold”). The former is from प्र- (pra-, “pro-”) + कृष्टयति (kṛṣṭayati), from कृष् (kṛṣ, “to draw, lead, plough”), and the latter from प्र- (pra-, “pro-”) + गृह्णाति (gṛhṇāti, “to grab, seize”).

ex.u: اِس مَوسم میں آم جلدی پک جائیں گے
ex.e: In this weather the mangoes will ripen soon.

senses.g: to ripen — to harden, become firm
to ripen — to whiten due to age (of hair)

پکنا.e: From a verbal derivative of Sanskrit पक्व (pakvá, “ripe”) (whence پَکّا (pakkā, “ripe; firm”)). Cognate with Punjabi ਪੱਕਣਾ (pakkaṇā) / پَکّݨا (pakkṇā), Saraiki پَکَّݨ (pakkaṇ), Gujarati પાકવું (pākvũ), Nepali पाक्नु (pāknu), Bengali পাকা (paka).

senses.g: dish, cuisine; any cooked or prepared food

پگار.e: Borrowed from Portuguese pagar. In Pakistan, borrowed from Gujarati પગાર (pagār).

پگڑی.e: Diminutive of پاگ (pāg). Compare Sindhi پَڳَ (pag̠a).

پگلنا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi पघिलना (paghilanā), from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀧𑀖𑀺𑀮𑀤𑀺 (*paghiladi), from Sanskrit *प्रघिरति (*praghirati), from प्र- (pra-, “forward, forth”) + घृ (ghṛ, “to moisten”). Cognate with Punjabi پِگَھلݨا (pighalṇā) / ਪਿਘਲਣਾ (pighlaṇā), Marathi पाघळणे (pāghaḷṇe).

پلاٹینم.e: Borrowed from English platinum, ultimately from Spanish platina.

پلک.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پلک (palk, palak).

پلیٹ.e: Borrowed from English plate from Old French plate, from Vulgar Latin *plat(t)us, from Ancient Greek πλατύς (platús, “broad, flat”).

senses.g: a man carrying water-pots on his head

ex.u: اتہاس کے پنا
ex.e: the pages of history

پنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀡𑁆𑀡 (paṇṇa), from Sanskrit पर्ण (parṇa).

پناہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَنَاه (panāh). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi پناہ (pnah /⁠panāh⁠/).

senses.g: vocative plural of پَناہ (panāh, “refuge; shelter; asylum”)
oblique plural of پَناہ (panāh, “refuge; shelter; asylum”)
direct plural of پَناہ (panāh, “refuge; shelter; asylum”)
Punjab (a geographical region in South Asia, politically divided between India and Pakistan)

پنجاہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پنجاه (panjāh). Compare Sanskrit पञ्चाशत् (pañcāśat).

senses.g: alternative form of پنج شیر (panj śer, “Panjshir”)

پنجہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَنْجَه (panja). Doublet of پان٘چ (pā̃c, “five”).

پنچھی.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit पक्षिन् (pakṣín, “bird”, literally “winged”). First attested as Old Hindi पंषी (pãṣī). Doublet of پَکْشِی (pakśī).

پندرہ.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa पण्णरह (paṇṇaraha), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀡𑁆𑀡𑀭𑀲 (paṇṇarasa), from Sanskrit पञ्चदश (páñcadaśa).

senses.g: canopy; pavilion; the ground of a large event or convention usually with tents or marquees etc.

پنڈت.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit पण्डित (paṇḍita). Doublet of پَنْڈا (panḍā).

پنکھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀓𑁆𑀔 (pakkha), from Sanskrit पक्ष (pakṣá).

ex.u: پُرانا پَن٘کھا کام نَہِیں کَرْتا۔
ex.e: The old fan doesn't work.

پنکھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀓𑁆𑀔 (pakkha), from Sanskrit पक्ष (pakṣa, “wing”)

senses.g: punkahwallah: a servant who operates a fan

پنکھڑی.e: From Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀧𑀁𑀓𑁆𑀔 (*paṃkkha) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀟- (-ḍa-) + -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-), from the nominative singular form of Sanskrit पक्ष्मन् (pákṣman). Compare Gujarati પાંખડી (pā̃khḍī), Marathi पाकळी (pākḷī).

senses.g: stair or quay for drawing water

ex.u: ... موہے پَنْگَھٹ پے نَنْد لال چھیڑ گَئیو رے ...
ex.e: ...Oh on the river quay, Nandlāl has teased me..

senses.g: boon, godsend, as a result of virtue
pope (head of the Roman Catholic Church)

پوت.e: Inherited from Old Hindi पूत (pūta), from Prakrit पुत्त (putta), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀢 (puta /⁠putta⁠/), 𑀧𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀭 (putra), from Sanskrit पुत्र (putrá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *putrás (“son”), from Proto-Indo-European *peh₂w- (“small, little, few”). Cognate with Avestan 𐬞𐬎𐬚𐬭𐬀 (puθra), Old Persian 𐎱𐎢𐏂 (p-u-ç /⁠puça⁠/, “son”), Ancient Greek παῖς (paîs, “child, son”), Latin puer (“boy”), Old English fēaw (whence English few). Doublet of پُتْر (putr) and پِسَر (pisar).

Inherited from Sanskrit पूत (pūta), in turn inherited from Proto-Indo-European *puH-tó-s (“pure, clean”), from *pewH- (“to purify”). Cognate with Latin putus and pūrus. Compare also English pure, a Latin borrowing.

پوتا.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi پوتا (pvta), from Prakrit *𑀧𑁄𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀅 (*pŏttaa), from Ashokan Prakrit 𑀧𑁄𑀢𑀓 (potaka /⁠pottaka⁠/), from Sanskrit पौत्र (páutra) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-).

senses.g: female equivalent of پوتا (potā): son's daughter, paternal grandaughter

پوتی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑁄𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀺𑀆 (pŏttiā), from Sanskrit पौत्री (pautrī) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀓𑀸- (-ikā-).

پوتھی.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀢𑁆𑀣𑀺𑀬𑀸 (putthiyā), from Sanskrit *पोस्तक (*postaka), पुस्तिका (pustikā).

ex.u: لوگ میری آگ جیسے جذبے کی پوجا کرتے ہیں
ex.r: log merī āg jaise jazbe kī pūjā karte ha͠i
ex.e: People worship my fiery spirit

senses.g: puja, a Hindu ritual offered to various deities, distinguished persons, or special guests.

ex.u: چھوڑوں گا میں نہ اس بتِ کافر کا بغیر
ex.r: choṛūṅgā ma͠i na us but-e-kāfir kā pūjnā
ex.e: I will not leave off the worship of that idol of the kafirs

پوجنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀇 (pujjaï), from Sanskrit पूज्यते (pūjyate, “to be worshiped”, passive form). Related to پُوجا (pūjā, “worship, veneration”).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀇 (pujjaï), from Sanskrit पूर्यते (pūryate, “to be filled”, passive form).

ex.u: میں آپ سے کُچْھ پُوچْھنا چاہتی ہوں.
ex.r: ma͠i āp se kuch pūchnā cāhtī hū̃.
ex.e: I would like to ask you something.

پوچھنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀘𑁆𑀙𑀤𑀺 (pucchadi), from Sanskrit पृच्छति (pṛccháti), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *pr̥šćáti, from Proto-Indo-European *pr̥sḱéti. Cognate with Gujarati પૂછવું (pūchvũ), Marathi पुसणे (pusṇe), Kalasha phúčik, Romani pućhel, Persian پرسیدن (porsīdan), German forschen.

پودا.e: Deaspirated variant of پَودھا (paudhā), from Sanskrit प्रवृद्ध (pravṛddha).

ex.u: پورے آسمان میں راکھ پھیل گئی تھی
ex.r: pūre āsamān mẽ rākh phail gayī thī
ex.e: Ash had spread across the whole sky.

ex.u: یہ کہانی پوری ہے
ex.e: This story is complete

ex.u: میں تمھارے درخواست کو پورا کر دوں گا
ex.r: ma͠i tumhāre darxvāst ko pūrā kardũ ga
ex.e: I will fulfill your request [lit. make it fulfilled]

پورا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀽𑀭𑀬 (pūraya, “filling”), from Sanskrit पूरक (pūraka, “filling”).

پوست.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پوسْت (pōst, “skin, hide, rind, shell, cover, poppy-head”), from Old Persian 𐎱𐎠𐎺𐎿𐎫𐎠 (p-a-v-s-t-a /⁠pavastā⁠/), ultimately from Proto-Indo-Iranian *pawástam (“covering, envelope”). Cognate to Gujarati પોસ (pos). See also Sanskrit पुस्तक (pustaka).

ex.u: ہِندُوسْتَانِی پوشَاک اَکْثَر رَنْگِیلِی ہوتِی ہَے۔
ex.r: hindūstānī pośāk aksar rangīlī hotī hai.
ex.e: Indian clothing is often colourful.

پوشیدگی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پوشِیدَگِی (pōšīdagī). By surface analysis, پوشِیدَہ (pośīda, “secret, hidden, concealed”) + گِی (gī). First attested in c. 1799 as Middle Hindi پوشِیدَگِی (pvśydgy).

پوشیدہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پوشِیدَه (pōšīda, “covered, hidden”).

پول.e: Inherited from Prakrit पोल्ल (pŏlla), from Proto-Dravidian *po-.
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀤𑁄𑀮𑀻 (padolī), from Sanskrit प्रतोली (pratolī).

senses.g: Paul (of Tarsus), author of several books of the New Testament
a male given name from Ancient Greek, equivalent to English Paul

پولس.e: From Ancient Greek Παῦλος (Paûlos), from Latin Paulus.

senses.g: Poland (a country in the Eastern Europe, Europe)
a male given name, Pavan, from Sanskrit

پونچھ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀼𑀁𑀙 (puṃcha), from Sanskrit पुच्छ (púccha), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *púsćas, from Proto-Indo-European *puḱsos. First attested as Old Hindi پونچھ (pvnch). Cognate with Punjabi پُچّھ (pucch) / ਪੁੱਛ (puccha).

senses.g: Poonch (a town, a district of Jammu, Jammu and Kashmir, India)
Poonch (a district, a division of Azad Jammu and Kashmir, Pakistan)
Poonch (a river in the autonomous territory of Azad Kashmir in Pakistan and the union territory of Jammu and Kashmir in India)
Poonch (a district in the princely state of Jammu and Kashmir)
Poonchh (a village in Moth tehsil, Jhansi district, Uttar Pradesh, India)

پونچھ.e: From Sanskrit पर्णोत्स (parṇotsa). Compare Kashmiri پرٛوٗنٛژھ (prūnċh).

پونچھنا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit प्रोञ्छति (proñchati). Cognate with Marathi पुसणे (pusṇe), Assamese মচা (mosa).

پہ.e: Colloquial and regional variant of پر. Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀭𑀺 (pari), 𑀉𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀭𑀺 (uppari), from Sanskrit उपरि (upari, “above”).

پہاڑ.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀧𑀸𑀳𑀸𑀟 (*pāhāḍa), possibly from Sanskrit पाषाण (pāṣāṇa). Cognate with Punjabi پَہاڑ (pahāṛ) / ਪਹਾੜ (pahāṛ), Nepali पहाड (pahāḍ), Gujarati પહાડ (pahāḍ), Bengali পাহাড় (pahaṛ), Assamese পাহাৰ (pahar), Sylheti ꠙꠣꠀꠠ (faaṛ).

پہچان.e: Inherited from Old Hindi पहिचान (pahicāna), from Sanskrit प्रत्यभिज्ञान (pratyabhijñāna). Related to پہچاننا.

پہچاننا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀘𑁆𑀘𑀳𑀺𑀬𑀸𑀡𑀇 (paccahiyāṇaï), from Sanskrit प्रत्यभिजानाति (pratyabhijānāti).

senses.g: unit of time equalling three hours
to take the first step; lead to initiate; to be the first one

پہلا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Apabhramsa पहिल (pahila), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀳𑀺𑀮 (pahila), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀧𑁆𑀭𑀣𑀺𑀮𑁆𑀮 (*prathilla). Sanskrit प्रथम (prathamá, “first”) was reanalyzed as having an ordinal suffix -म (-ma), which was replaced by Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀇𑀮𑁆𑀮- (-illa-). Also cognate to now-obsolete Classical Persian فَرْدُم (fardum, “first”). Doublet of فَرْدُم (fardum).

senses.g: inflection of پَہلا (“first”): — direct masculine plural
inflection of پَہلا (“first”): — oblique masculine singular/plural

ex.u: ہَوَائی جَہاز دُبَئی تَک پَہُن٘چا ہَے۔
ex.r: havāī jahāz dubaī tak pahũcā hai.
ex.e: The airplane has reached as far as Dubai.

ex.u: اُس کو بَہُت تَکْلِیف پَہُن٘چِی۔
ex.e: He was very hurt.

پہنچنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀳𑀼𑀘𑁆𑀘 (pahucca) + ـنا (-nā, verbal suffix).

پہننا.e: From a metathesis of Old Hindi पिन्हना (pinhanā), from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *पिणहइ (piṇahaï), from Sanskrit पिनह्यति (pinahyati, “wears, ties on, fastens”). Cognate with Punjabi ਪਹਿਨਣਾ (pahinṇā).

پہیا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀇𑀅 (païa), from Sanskrit प्रधि (pradhí).

senses.g: initialism of پْریس ٹْرَسْٹ آف اِنْڈِیا (pres ṭrasṭ āf inḍiyā)
initialism of پاکِسْتان تَحْرِیکِ اِنْصَاف (pākistān tahrīk-i insāf)
one on foot; footman, footsoldier, infantryman

ex.u: ہَمارے جاں باز پیادے دُشْمَن پَر بھاری پَڑ رَہیں ہَیں۔
ex.r: hamāre jā̃ bāz pyāde duśman par bhārī paṛ rahẽ ha͠i.
ex.e: Our undaunted infantrymen are bearing hard on the enemy.

ex.u: پِچْھلی چال میں وہ میرا آخری پیادَہ کَھا گَیا۔
ex.r: pichlī cāl mẽ vo merā āxrī pyāda khā gayā.
ex.e: He captured my last pawn on the previous move.

senses.g: affection; attachment (commonly with inanimate objects, or connoted with feelings)

پیار.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi پیار (pyar) (c. 1611), Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀺𑀆𑀭 (piāra), from Sanskrit प्रियकार (priyakāra), compound of प्रिय (priyá) + कार (kāra).

پیارا.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa 𑀧𑀺𑀬𑀸𑀭𑀬 (piyāraya-, “dear”), Sanskrit प्रियकारा (priyakārā, “agreeable”), from प्रिय (priya) + कार (kāra). By surface analysis, پیار (pyār) + ـا.
Borrowed from Portuguese pera (“pear”). Cognate with Bengali পেয়ারা (peẏara), Malayalam പേര (pēra), Gujarati પેરુ (peru), Marathi पेरू (perū), Swahili pera.

ex.u: کیا تمھیں پیاس لگی ہے؟
ex.e: Are you thirsty?

پیاس.e: Inherited from Apabhramsa पिआस (piāsa), from Prakrit 𑀧𑀺𑀯𑀸𑀲𑀸 (pivāsā), from Sanskrit पिपासा (pipāsā).

پیالہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پِیَالَه (piyāla), from Middle Persian [script needed] (pygʾl /⁠*paygāl⁠/). First attested as Old Hindi पियाला (piyālā).

senses.g: bodhi tree, sacred fig, peepal, Ficus religiosa, the tree under which the Buddha gained enlightenment

پیپل.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀺𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀮 (pippala), from Sanskrit पिप्पल (pippala).

پیتل.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀺𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀮 (pittala), from Sanskrit पित्तल (pittala).

پیٹ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit पेट (peṭa, “bag, basket”), from the root verb पिट् (piṭ, “to collect or heap together”).

senses.g: solemn declaration, agreement, engagement, promise, vow

پیج.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀇𑀚𑁆𑀚𑀸 (païjjā), from Sanskrit प्रतिज्ञा (pratijñā). Doublet of پرتِگّیا (pratigyā). Cognate with Marathi पैज (paij).

پیچ کس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پیچ کش (pēč-kaš).

پیدا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پیدا (paidā). Compare Kashmiri پٲدٕ (pạ̄dụ).

ex.u: یہ غریبی سرکاری بدعنوانی کی پیدائش ہیں.
ex.e: This poverty is a genesis of governmental corruption.

پیر.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀧𑀬 (paya) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀭- (-ra-).

senses.g: Paris (the capital and largest city of France)

پیرس.e: Borrowed from English Paris. Compare Punjabi پَیْرَس (pairas).

پیرو.e: Borrowed from Portuguese peru. Alternatively, from Classical Persian پِیل مُرْغ (pīl murġ, literally “elephant chicken”) (compare Dari فِیلمُرْغ (fīl-murġ)), thus possibly a doublet of فِیل مُرْغ (fīl murġ).

پیروزہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پیروزَہ (pērōza), from Middle Persian *pērōzag.

پیڑ.e: Inherited from Proto-Indo-Aryan *peḍ-. Cognate with Punjabi پیڑ (peṛ).

senses.g: paisa (a subdivision of currency, equal to ¹⁄₁₀₀ of a rupee)

پیسہ.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi پیسا (pysa) (c. 1528), from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀧𑀬𑀁𑀲 (*payaṃsa), from Sanskrit *पदांश (*padāṃśa, “quarter part”), from पद (pada, “quarter”) + अंश (aṃśa, “part, piece”), in reference to the coin being worth a quarter of an anna.

senses.g: inflection of پَیسَہ (paisa, “money; cash”): — direct plural
inflection of پَیسَہ (paisa, “money; cash”): — oblique/vocative singular

پیشاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پیشاب (pēšāb, literally “front-water”).

senses.g: alternative form of پیش گوئِی (peś goī)

پیشہ ور.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پیشه ور.

پیغمبر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پَیْغَمْبَر (payġambar), from Middle Persian pgtʾmbl (payğāmbar).

پیل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian پیل (pīl). Cognate with Pashto پيل (pil).

پیلا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀺𑀢𑁆𑀢𑀮 (pittala), from Sanskrit पीतल (pītala, “brass, yellow”).

senses.g: agreement, compact, convention, treaty, stipulation, pledge, promise; security; confirmation; asseveration, oath
to take (medication in liquid / oral solution)
to drink (any type of alcohol or liquor)

پینا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀧𑀺𑀩𑀇 (pibaï), 𑀧𑀺𑀯𑀇 (pivaï), from Sanskrit पिबति (pibati, “to drink, to exhaust, to suck, to swallow”), from Proto-Indo-Aryan *píbati, from Proto-Indo-Iranian *píbati, from Proto-Indo-European *píbeti, late variant of *píph₃eti, reduplication of *peh₃- (“to drink”). Cognates include Assamese পিয়া (pia), Dhivehi ބޮނީ (bonī), Gujarati પીવું (pīvũ), Kachchi પીણૂ (pīṇū), Marathi पिणे (piṇe), Nepali पिउनु (piunu), Odia ପିଇବା (piibā), Punjabi پینا / ਪੀਣਾ (pīṇā), Romani pijel, Sindhi پِئَڻُ (piaṇu), and Sinhalese බොනවා (bonawā).

senses.g: the barrier of a railway crossing; level crossing
a frontier gate, marking international borders

ex.u: واہگہ پر وہ دروازہ موجود ہے‘ جس کے ایک طرف کے پھاٹک بھارت میں اور دوسری طرف کے پاکستان میں کھلتے ہیں۔
ex.r: vāhga par vo darvāza maujūd hai jis ke ek tarf ke phāṭak bhārat mẽ aur dūsrī tarf ke pākistān mẽ khulte ha͠i.
ex.e: There is a gate at [the] Wagah [border] whose [frontier] gates on one side open into India and on the other side into Pakistan.

senses.g: the bar of a court of justice; pound

پھاٹک.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi پهاٿک (phaṭk /⁠phāṭak⁠/), from Sanskrit *फाट्टक्क (*phāṭṭakka), compound of *प्रहट्ट (*prahaṭṭa) + हट्ट (haṭṭa).

senses.g: a slice or piece of fruit
spade (garden tool with a handle and a flat blade for digging)
to be seeing an individual (ie. in a relationship)

پھرنا.e: Urdu verb set پِھرْنا (phirnā) پھیرْنا (phernā) پِھرانا (phirānā) پِھرْوانا (phirvānā) Etymology tree Sanskrit स्पृ (spṛ)der.? Proto-Middle Indo-Aryan *𑀨𑀺𑀭𑀢𑀺 (*phirati) Prakrit 𑀨𑀺𑀭𑀇 (phiraï) Apabhramsa 𑆦𑆴𑆫𑆅 (phiraï) Urdu پِھرْنا Inherited from Middle Hindi پھرْنا (phrna /⁠phirnā⁠/) (c. 1606), from Old Hindi फिरना (phiranā) (c. 1400s), from Apabhramsa 𑆦𑆴𑆫𑆅 (phiraï), from Prakrit 𑀨𑀺𑀭𑀇 (phiraï, “goes, returns”), from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀨𑀺𑀭𑀢𑀺 (*phirati, “moves, wanders, turns”).

پھل.e: Inherited from Middle Hindi پھل (phl /⁠phal⁠/) (c. 1518), from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀨𑀮 (phala), from Sanskrit फल (phála), borrowed from Proto-Dravidian *paẓam (“ripe fruit”).

senses.g: to break out as blisters or pimples

پھلنا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit फलति (phalati) or from پھل (phal). Cognate with Punjabi ਫਲਣਾ (phalṇā) / پَھلݨا (phalṇā), Sindhi ڦَلَڻُ (phalaṇu) / फलणु, Gujarati ફળવું (phaḷvũ), Nepali फल्नु (phalnu), Marathi फळणे (phaḷṇe), Bengali ফলা (phola).

پھوپھا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀨𑀼𑀧𑁆𑀨𑀼 (*phupphu), from baby talk.

senses.g: a boil, sore, ulcer, abscess, tumour

پھوڑا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀨𑁄𑀟 (phoḍa), from Sanskrit स्फोट (sphoṭa). Cognate with Punjabi ਫੋੜਾ (phoṛā) / پھوڑا (phoṛā), Marathi फोड (phoḍ), Konkani फोड (phoḍ), Sinhalese පොළ (poḷa).

پھول.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀨𑀼𑀮𑁆𑀮 (phulla), from Sanskrit फुल्ल (phulla). Cognates include Assamese ফুল (phul), Bengali ফুল (phul), Bhojpuri फूल (phūl), Gujarati ફૂલ (phūl), Maithili फूल (phūl), Marathi फूल (phūl), Nepali फूल (phūl), Odia ଫୁଲ (phula), Punjabi پھل / ਫੁੱਲ (phulla), and Sindhi ڦل (phulu).

پھونکنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀨𑀼𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀤𑀺 (phukkadi), from Sanskrit *फूत्कति (*phūtkati), extended from the root फूत् (phūt) or perhaps a back-formation from फूत्कार (phūtkāra, “blowing”). Cognate with Nepali फुक्नु (phuknu), Gujarati ફૂંકવું (phū̃kvũ), Marathi फुंकणे (phuṅkṇe).

ex.u: جاؤ اعطراف کا ایک پھیرا لگا آؤ۔
ex.r: jā'o i‘t̤irāf kā aik pherā lagā ā'o.
ex.e: Go circle the area.

پھیرا.e: Inherited from Ashokan Prakrit *𑀨𑁂𑀭 (*phera) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀓- (-ka-).

پھینکنا.e: Inherited from Proto-New Indo-Aryan *𑀨𑁂𑀓𑁆𑀓𑀇 (*phekkaï). Cognate with Gujarati ફેંકવું (pheṅkvũ), Marathi फेकणे (phekṇe).

senses.g: The fourth letter of the Urdu alphabet, written in the Arabic script; preceded by پ and followed by ٹ.
Forming abstract nouns denoting a state, condition, or quality.

ex.u: چاہ (cāh, “liking, wish”) + ت (t) → چاہت (cāhat, “desire, fondness”)

تا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تا (tā), from Middle Persian [script needed] (OD /⁠tā⁠/, “until, so that”), from Old Persian 𐎹𐎠𐎫𐎠 (y-a-t-a /⁠yātā⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian تا (tā), from Middle Persian [script needed] (OD /⁠tā⁠/, “until, so that”), from Old Persian 𐎹𐎠𐎫𐎠 (y-a-t-a /⁠yātā⁠/).
Borrowed from Classical Persian تا (tā), from Middle Persian [script needed] (OD /⁠tā⁠/, “until, so that”), from Old Persian 𐎹𐎠𐎫𐎠 (y-a-t-a /⁠yātā⁠/).

تابندہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تابنده (tābinda, tābanda).

تابوت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تابُوت, from Arabic تَابُوت (tābūt).

تابہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تابه (tāba, “frying pan”), related to تاب (tāb, “heat”); cognate with Urdu توا (tavā).

تاپ.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit ताप (tāpá), from Proto-Indo-Iranian *tāpás, from Proto-Indo-European *tep-.

تاپنا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀢𑀧𑁆𑀧𑀇 (tappaï), from Sanskrit तप्य॑ति (tápyati). Cognate with Punjabi ਤਪਣਾ (tapṇā) / تَپݨا (tapṇā), Sindhi تَپَڻُ (tapaṇu) / तपणु, Gujarati તાપવું (tāpvũ), Marathi तापणे (tāpṇe), Sinhalese තපිනවා (tapinawā).

senses.g: Tatarstan (a republic and federal subject of south-central Russia)

تاتارستان.e: Borrowed from Tatar Татарстан (Tatarstan) and/or Classical Persian تَاتَارِسْتَان (tātāristān). By surface analysis, تَاتَار (“Tatar”) + ـسْتَان (“-stan”).

تاثیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تَأْثِیر (ta'sīr), borrowed from Arabic تَأْثِير (taʔṯīr), from أَثَّرَ (ʔaṯṯara). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi تاثیر (tas̱yr /⁠tāsīr⁠/).

senses.g: Taj Mahal (a monument in Agra, India, constructed between 1631 and 1654, considered one of the finest examples of Mughal architecture in the world)

تار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تَار (tār). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi تار (tar /⁠tār⁠/).

تارا.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit तारा (tārā). Doublet of سِتارَہ (sitāra).

تارکین.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تارکین (tārikīn), from the Arabic plural of تَارِك (tārik), active participle of تَرَكَ (taraka, “to leave”).

ex.u: آج کْیا تارِیخ ہَے؟
ex.e: What date is it today?

تاریخ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تاریخ (tārix), from Arabic تَارِيخ (tārīḵ).

تاریخچہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian تَارِیخْچَه (tārīxča), diminutive of تَارِیخ (tārīx).

تاریخی.e: Borrowed from Arabic تَارِيخِيّ (tārīḵiyy) (likely through Classical Persian تاریخی (tārīxī)). By surface analysis, تاریخ (tārīx, “history”) + ـی (-ī).

تاڑ.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀢𑀸𑀟 (tāḍa), from Sanskrit ताड (tāḍa).

تاڑی.e: From تاڑ (tāṛ, “palmyra, toddy palm”) + ـی (-ī), ultimately from Dravidian.

ex.u: تازہ خبر
ex.e: breaking news

senses.g: inflection of تَازہ (“fresh”): — direct masculine plural
inflection of تَازہ (“fresh”): — oblique masculine singular/plural
slapping the hands on the arms in defiance
